data.title: 30-Year Treasury Yield Rises to 4.962% This Week — Data Talk
data.description:
The 30-year yield rose 0.147 percentage point to 4.962% this week. The price is 92 27/32. 




     --Largest one-week yield gain since Friday, Dec. 13, 2024 



     --Yield is up for five consecutive weeks 



     --Yield is up 0.632 percentage point over the last five weeks 



     --Largest five-week yield gain since the week ending Oct. 20, 2023 



     --Longest streak of rising yields since the week ending Oct. 6, 2023, when the yield rose for five straight weeks 



     --Today it is up 0.042 percentage point and the price fell 20/32 price points 



     --Yield is up nine of the past 11 trading days 



     --A new 52-week high 



     --Highest yield since Wednesday, Nov. 1, 2023, 



     --Yield is up 1.026 percentage points from its 52-week low of 3.936% hit Monday, Sept. 16, 2024

data.title: Georgia Power responding to Winter Storm Cora Friday
data.description:
Georgia Power responding to Winter Storm Cora Friday

Georgia Power responding to Winter Storm Cora Friday

PR Newswire

ATLANTA, Jan. 10, 2025

Crews prepared, working as winter weather continues with freezing rain and ice today;
Company reminds customers to stay safe and informed

ATLANTA, Jan. 10, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- As a significant winter weather system swept across the state Friday, Georgia Power crews are prepared and responding to issues quickly, and as conditions are safe to work. Winter Storm Cora continues to make its way through the state Friday, with additional freezing rain and icy conditions expected to follow the several inches of snow that have fallen across north Georgia today.

As of midday Friday, power interruptions to customers have been minimal, but may increase with additional ice accumulation, falling trees or weather-related causes. All Georgia Power resources are engaged and well positioned and, in addition to local crews, the company has moved crews from South Georgia to reinforce efforts where needed, with additional personnel available from within the Southern Company system and Florida.

Focused on Reliability Every Day
Whether for winter storms like Cora or "blue-sky days," Georgia Power is always focused on improving reliability and adding resiliency to the power grid. The company has invested heavily in infrastructure improvements in recent years to bolster resiliency through equipment upgrades as well as the strategic implementation of Smart Grid technology. This equipment and related systems enhance the company's real-time ability to identify and quickly isolate outages that occur, as well as remotely restore or reroute power around damage. This technology helps minimize those affected even before crews begin repair work in the field.

Safety Tips for Customers and Crews
Georgia Power urges customers to prioritize safety during severe winter weather and provides the following tools to stay connected and informed.

  • Watch for Georgia Power crews working across the state. If you must be on the roads, please move over one lane for utility vehicles stopped on the side of the road – it's the law in Georgia.
  • Never touch any downed or low-hanging wire. Warn those going out to play in snow or clear debris to watch for and avoid downed wires.
  • Never pull tree limbs off of power lines yourself or enter areas with heavy snow, debris or downed trees as downed power lines may be concealed. Customers should call 911 or Georgia Power immediately if they see a fallen or low-hanging power line.
  • Take care if using a portable generator. Follow all manufacturer's instructions, avoid using generators in enclosed spaces and be mindful of electrical safety. Click here for more safety tips.

Stay Connected & Informed:

  • Outage Alerts – Customers subscribed to our free Georgia Power Outage Alert service will receive personalized notifications and updates via text message. Check that your contact number is up to date to receive the latest information.
  • Social Media – Follow Georgia Power across Facebook, Instagram and X for storm tips, outage updates, customer service and more.
  • Outage & Storm Center – Available at www.GeorgiaPower.com/Storm, customers can visit this site to check their contact information is updated to receive Outage Alerts, report and check the status of outages, and access useful safety tips and information. Customers can also report and check the status of an outage 24 hours a day by contacting Georgia Power at 888-891-0938.
  • Outage Map – Housed within the Outage & Storm Center, Georgia Power's interactive Outage Map provides near real-time information, allowing users to see where outages are occurring across the state and track estimated restoration times. The map is updated regularly from teams in the field.
  • Georgia Power Mobile App – Download the Georgia Power mobile app for Apple and Android devices to access storm and outage information on the go.

About Georgia Power
Georgia Power is the largest electric subsidiary of Southern Company (NYSE: SO), America's premier energy company. Value, Reliability, Customer Service and Stewardship are the cornerstones of the company's promise to 2.7 million customers in all but four of Georgia's 159 counties. Committed to delivering clean, safe, reliable and affordable energy, Georgia Power maintains a diverse, innovative generation mix that includes nuclear, coal and natural gas, as well as renewables such as solar, hydroelectric and wind. Georgia Power focuses on delivering world-class service to its customers every day and the company is recognized by J.D. Power as an industry leader in customer satisfaction. For more information, visit www.GeorgiaPower.com and connect with the company on Facebook (Facebook.com/GeorgiaPower), X (X.com/GeorgiaPower) and Instagram (Instagram.com/ga_power).

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/georgia-power-responding-to-winter-storm-cora-friday-302348332.html

SOURCE Georgia Power

data.title: Lynch Carpenter Investigates Claims in PowerSchool Data Breach
data.description:
PITTSBURGH, Jan. 10, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- PowerSchool is a cloud-based solutions provider for K-12 schools and school districts. PowerSchool recently confirmed that it suffered from a cybersecurity incident, which may have impacted the personal information of students, parents, and guardians. The information potentially impacted in the data breach includes individuals’ names and addresses, and in some cases, information including Social Security Numbers, information, and grades.

Lynch Carpenter, LLP is investigating claims against PowerSchool related to this data breach. If you received a data breach notification from PowerSchool, or your child’s school or school district, you may be entitled to compensation.

If you have received a notification from PowerSchool, or your child’s school or school district which states that you or your child’s information was impacted, please call Patrick Donathen at (412) 322-9243, email him at patrick@lcllp.com, or fill out our contact form at www.lynchcarpenter.com/contact.

About Lynch Carpenter

Lynch Carpenter is a national class action law firm with offices in Pennsylvania, California, and Illinois. Our firm has represented millions of clients in data privacy matters for more than a decade and has earned national acclaim for complex litigation for plaintiffs across the country. To learn more, please visit www.lynchcarpenter.com.

CONTACT Patrick Donathen
COMPANY Lynch Carpenter LLP
PHONE (412) 322-9243
EMAIL patrick@lcllp.com
WEB lynchcarpenter.com

data.title: CUPE Ontario and OMW Join AMO’s, Municipalities’ Calls to Deal With Human and Financial Costs of Ontario’s Homelessness Crisis
data.description:
CUPE Ontario and OMW Join AMO’s, Municipalities’ Calls to Deal With Human and Financial Costs of Ontario’s Homelessness Crisis

In response to the report “Municipalities under pressure: The human and financial cost of Ontario’s homelessness crisis,” released by the Association of Municipalities of Ontario, CUPE Ontario and its Ontario Municipal Workers committee (OMW) issued the following statement:

With both a federal and provincial election in the offing, CUPE Ontario and OMW recognize the importance of AMO and municipalities having added their voices to underscore the crisis of homelessness and propose solutions to help end the crisis in homelessness.

However, our members know all too well how we arrived at this crisis: for decades, successive governments have treated housing only as a for-profit commodity, instead of actively investing to help guarantee housing as a necessity and a human right.

As a result, large corporate landlords treat housing as their own cash cow, and housing costs have spiralled. At the same time, the Ford Conservatives slashed protections like rent controls that kept housing affordable and allowed greedy corporate investors to dominate the rental market.

Meanwhile, federal and provincial governments have abandoned the construction of affordable social housing. They have focused on helping developers – an approach that has failed to deliver the deeply affordable housing Ontarians need, and that may even have deepened the crisis. All the while, governments continue to scapegoat immigrants, refugees, and the unhoused themselves for a crisis in homelessness that these groups played no part in creating.

With the right kind of investment, we could stop the financialization of housing and take it out of the hands of profiteers. We need to take back control of housing in Ontario. As part of a suite of direct government action to deal with homelessness, we must invest and build not-for-profit, affordable, public housing and reinstitute strong rent controls.

We’ve learned the hard way that a vote for Doug Ford and the Conservatives won’t achieve these much-needed measures. A vote for Conservatives will only yield more homelessness and more poverty.

To protect housing, CUPE members will work to put NDP governments in Queen’s Park and Ottawa.

MU:cj/cope491

For more information, contact
Mary Unan, CUPE Communications, 647-390-9839 | munan@cupe.ca

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250110917622/en/

data.title: High five or high dive? What we know about stock returns in years ending in '5.'
data.description:
By Mark Hulbert

History suggests '5' is the market's lucky number - just don't bet on it

There's a widely held theory that the U.S. stock market has a high likelihood of rising in 2025 - for no other reason than where it falls in the decade.

In truth, years ending in "5" are no better for the stock market than any other year.

My hunch is that Wall Street brokers are grasping at straws to find a rationale that could justify their clients investing more money in stocks. This took on new urgency after the "Santa Claus rally" failed, and then again when the stock market struggled during the first five trading days of January - a period, that per the "first five days of January" indicator, foretells the market's full-year direction.

That said, there does appear to be strong support for the notion that years ending in the number 5 are good for the stock market. In the 13 such years since 1895, according to one chart that several readers have sent me, the stock market has risen 92.3% of the time. The only one with a decline was the most recent 5-ending year, 2015. Since across all calendar years, the stock market rises about two-thirds of the time, this 92.3% gain rate certainly seems significant.

To know whether you should risk your money on a continuation of this pattern, you should subject it to what's known as an out-of-sample test. And such a test is readily available, courtesy of a database compiled by Edward McQuarrie, professor emeritus at the Leavey School of Business at Santa Clara University in California.

McQuarrie's database extends back to the early 1790s, which means it contains 10 additional 5-ending years than the sample that begins in 1895. Of those 10 years, the stock market rose in five of them. That's worse than the average gain across all calendar years.

The chart above reports each year's gain rate over the entire period since 1794. Notice that while 5-ending years have an above-average gain rate, two other years - those ending in "8" or "9" - have even higher success rates. Regardless, none of the differences shown in the chart is significant at the 95% confidence level that statisticians often use when assessing if a pattern is genuine.

Another sign that the "years ending in 5" theory is not worth following in your investments is that there is no plausible explanation for why it should work. That's a revealing deficiency, since if you torture a database long enough you can always find apparent statistical regularities that have no real-world significance.

My favorite example, and there are many, is from David Leinweber, the former director of the Center for Innovative Financial Technology at the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory. In his book "Stupid Data Miner Tricks: Overfitting the S&P 500," he reported that one of the indicators with the strongest statistical correlation with the S&P 500 SPX is butter production in Bangladesh.

Mark Hulbert is a regular contributor to MarketWatch. His Hulbert Ratings tracks investment newsletters that pay a flat fee to be audited. He can be reached at mark@hulbertratings.com

More: Can January really tell the stock market's future? Let us count the ways.

Also read: S&P 500 starts January with gains after Santa rally busts. What to watch for next.

-Mark Hulbert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-10-25 1524ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Hometown First Responder: New Memoir THE 40 YEAR DEPLOYMENT: A FIRST RESPONDER’S JOURNEY Examines a Lifelong Career in Emergency Services
data.description:
Charleston, SC, Jan. 10, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Deploying from home to protect and serve their communities, first responders are, in fact, warriors. And while most people recognize the importance of honoring first responders, few can fully understand the impact of a career in emergency services. For as long as he can remember, Robert Ulrich felt his career in emergency services was somewhat like an armed services deployment. While his “war stories” took place within his own community, he, like many soldiers, discovered how trauma and success are intertwined. And after all the life-altering wins and losses of his nearly forty-year career, Ulrich revels in his hard-earned victory¾a broader perspective of the world. Embarking on a journey of introspection and self-discovery, Ulrich reflects on what he has learned in a new memoir.  

In “The 40 Year Deployment: A First Responder’s Journey,” Ulrich relates his career defining moments to educate and guide future generations of first responders. Illustrating how his career choice not only shaped his life but also the lives of those around him, Ulrich paints a picture of the long-term effects of working as a first responder. Exploring themes of self-awareness and personal growth, Ulrich teaches readers to be discerning, knowing when to lead and when to follow. A fascinating look at a day in the life of a fire service first responder, the book offers communities a better understanding of their own “hometown warriors” and is a must read for anyone considering entering the field of public service.

“The 40 Year Deployment: A First Responder’s Journey” is available for purchase on Amazon.com and BarnesandNoble.com. For every book sold, one dollar will be donated to Foundation 58, a non-profit providing support services to first responders.

About the Author:

Robert Bruce Ulrich is a career lieutenant in the fire service. Spanning nearly forty years, his career in emergency services began at the age of fifteen, and he has worked with various fire departments and in the private sector. Holding several professional certifications and licenses, he has taught classes at several fire academies, colleges, and universities. A proud father and grandfather, he lives in Andrews, South Carolina with his wife.

For more information about the author and his work, please follow him on Facebook or Instagram.

Media Contact:

Robert Ulrich

chief2106@gmail.com

Available for interviews: Author, Robert Bruce Ulrich

Attachment

  • The 40 Year Deployment: A First Responder’s Journey, 

Leah Joseph
Palmetto Publishing
publicity@palmettopublishing.com

data.title: Can I move money from my IRA to an HSA? 'Medical expenses keep growing.'
data.description:
By Alessandra Malito

'If there was a way to pay for them out of pretax funds, it would be most helpful'

Dear Help Me Retire,

My husband has been retired for 10 years and yes, he takes required minimum distributions from his IRA. I heard that in retirement you can set up a one-time disbursement to a health savings account from an IRA. Is this true?

Medical expenses keep growing, and if there was a way to pay for them out of pretax funds, it would be most helpful. Thank you.

Overwhelmed

See: I'm 57 with 7-year-old twins and a stay-at-home wife with $0 in her own retirement savings. We're focused on financial aid for the kids.

Dear Overwhelmed,

You're absolutely right. Medical expenses are constantly increasing and they can become quite overwhelming, especially as we get older.

The cost of healthcare in retirement jumped 5% last year, up to an average amount of $165,000 for a 65-year-old retiring that year, according to Fidelity Investments. That figure has more than doubled since 2002, the investment firm found. But Americans of all ages - almost half of U.S. adults - are struggling to pay for their medical expenses, according to the West Health-Gallup Healthcare Affordability Index.

Do you have questions about retirement, Social Security, where to live or how to afford it at all? We want to hear from you. Join the conversation in our Facebook community: Retire Better with MarketWatch.

The Internal Revenue Service does allow a one-time distribution from an IRA to a health savings account, but there are many, many rules tacked on.

This isn't your typical transfer of money, such as from a savings account to a checking account, or even a 401(k) to an IRA. Some investors may think of it as similar to a Roth conversion, but according to Brad Herdt, a certified financial planner at Deseret Mutual Benefit Administrators, "this doesn't behave at all like a Roth conversion."

For starters, this transfer is considered a contribution to the HSA, so you have to be eligible for an HSA in order to make the one-time distribution. In other words, you can't choose to put money into an old HSA just because you happen to have an IRA and an HSA. You also have to remain eligible for up to 12 months after the contribution, or the IRS might consider the transaction to be a taxable event. (This is especially important to note for people who are nearing Medicare eligibility and may switch over from a high-deductible health plan with an HSA.)

The amount transferred can be up to the contribution limits of the HSA. In 2025, those limits are $4,300 for individuals and $8,550 for families. As Herdt noted, it isn't treated like a conversion, which means that the amount you put into the HSA cannot exceed the annual limit. (Roth conversions, on the other hand, do not count toward annual contribution limits, allowing investors to put the maximum allowed into their IRA and then move over however much they'd like through a conversion.)

You also want to pick the right time, since this is a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity. One situation is when an individual has access to an HSA but can't afford to make contributions to it. Younger investors might benefit most from this strategy, since they would be able to capitalize on the compound interest, Herdt said.

But as you said, medical expenses do tend to grow, and paying for them with pretax funds is a huge benefit. Another opportune time to make this distribution would be when there's a big medical expense and the investor intends to pay for it with the money in their HSA.

"The IRS has granted this one-time exception because they do understand sometimes people can be hit with a medical issue," said Melissa Caro, a certified financial planner and founder of My Retirement Network. "The reason to note that it is almost considered an emergency, so you really want to make sure you're taking it seriously." You may also need to work with your financial adviser or call the investment firm housing your IRA or HSA to do the transfer, as it likely isn't possible to do on your own, she said.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

Readers: Do you have suggestions for this reader? Add them in the comments below.

Have a question about your own retirement savings? Email us at HelpMeRetire@marketwatch.com

-Alessandra Malito

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-10-25 1500ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
